evidence: RelationConfig.relationType() is declared as returning RelationType but GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() can return null; ExplorationHandlers lines 69 and 180 call .relationType().name() without a null guard, giving a concrete NPE path when a relation has no type field.

RelationConfig.getFullPattern() returns null when pattern is null (explicit null return in first guard), and CollocateProfileComparator.exploreMultiSeed() passes the result directly to executor.executeSurfacePattern(seed, bcqlPattern, ...) without checking for null; executeSurfacePattern has no null check on bcqlPattern before calling CorpusQueryLanguageParser.parse(bcqlPattern, ...).
ExplorationHandlers line 100 contains an unchecked downcast: ((Map<String, Object>) response.get(

)).put(...) — fetching a value from a Map<String,Object> as Object and casting it to Map<String,Object> suppresses the type check; the cast is safe in practice only because buildBaseExploreResponse always inserts the same type, but this is not enforced by the type system.

All API response construction throughout ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers uses Map<String, Object> — typed domain records (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge) are immediately dissolved into untyped maps via private format* helpers, so the shape of the JSON response is invisible to the type system.

BlackLabSnippetParser static helper methods (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch, extractCollocateLemma) return nullable String without any annotation or contract; callers in BlackLabQueryExecutor do guard consistently, but callers in new code could silently skip the check.

Positive: sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits clause (SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult) provides exhaustive type checking for pattern matching — correct use of Java's type system.
Positive: Optional<RelationConfig> returned from GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() is used correctly with .isEmpty() checks at every call site before .get().
Positive: record types used for immutable data (RelationConfig, HitRecord, KwicResult, SnippetResult, CollocateResult, ExploreParams) remove the risk of partial construction.
Positive: QueryExecutor interface uses precise generic return types throughout (List<QueryResults.WordSketchResult>, List<QueryResults.CollocateResult>) rather than raw List.

strengths: Sealed ConcordanceResult hierarchy (SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult) enables exhaustive pattern-matching and correct permits-based type checking.
Optional<RelationConfig> from GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() is used correctly — every call site checks isEmpty() before unwrapping.
Record types for immutable DTOs (RelationConfig, HitRecord, ExploreParams, the three ConcordanceResult subtypes) eliminate partial-construction risk.
QueryExecutor interface uses specific generic signatures throughout instead of raw types or Object returns.
type_safety.issue_character: The issues fall into two patterns: nullable fields not captured in the type contract leading to concrete NPE paths (relationType(), getFullPattern()), and a systemic choice to dissolve typed domain objects into Map<String, Object> at the API layer, making the response shape invisible to the type system.
type_safety.score_rationale: The domain model layer is well-typed — sealed interfaces, records, Optional, and precise generics are used correctly. However, two concrete null-propagation bugs exist in the production path (relationType().name() and getFullPattern() → executeSurfacePattern without null guard), and the entire API response layer erases type information by converting rich domain objects into Map<String, Object>. Against a scale where 80 = solid-but-uneven and 60 = significant drag, this codebase sits slightly below 'solid' because the nullable-without-contract pattern on a record component (relationType) is a structural dishonesty — the record promises a non-null enum but the system allows null — and the Map<String, Object> response pattern means runtime-only feedback on any structural mistake in the API shape.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.relationType() is nullable but callers invoke .name() without null guard, risking NPE
evidence: RelationConfig record declares 'RelationType relationType' as a component — the type signature implies non-null.
GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() returns null when the JSON field is absent or unrecognised (line ~204).

ExplorationHandlers lines 69 and 180: 'String relationType = relationConfig.get().relationType().name()' — no null check before .name(); if relationType is null this is a NPE on any relation without a type field.
issues.suggestion: Either add a null guard ('relationType() != null ? relationType().name() : "SURFACE"') at both call sites, or make the record compact constructor validate non-null and throw at load time — the latter is safer because it surfaces misconfiguration early.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.getFullPattern() can return null, which propagates to executeSurfacePattern without a null check
evidence: RelationConfig.getFullPattern(): 'if (pattern == null) return null' — explicit null return on first line.

CollocateProfileComparator.exploreMultiSeed(): 'String bcqlPattern = relationConfig.getFullPattern(seed); executor.executeSurfacePattern(seed, bcqlPattern, ...)' — no null check between assignment and use.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern(): no null check on bcqlPattern before 'CorpusQueryLanguageParser.parse(bcqlPattern, "lemma")' — will throw NullPointerException if bcqlPattern is null.
issues.suggestion: Return Optional<String> from getFullPattern() to make nullability explicit, or add a precondition check in executeSurfacePattern ('if (bcqlPattern == null) return Collections.emptyList()') and add a guard in exploreMultiSeed before the call.

issues.summary: API response layer systematically uses Map<String, Object> instead of typed DTOs, hiding response shape from the type system
evidence: All format* helpers in ExplorationHandlers (formatSeedCollocate, formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatEdge, formatAdjectiveProfile, buildBaseExploreResponse) return Map<String, Object>, dissolving typed domain records.

The unchecked cast on line 100, '((Map<String, Object>) response.get("parameters")).put("nouns_per", nounsPerCollocate)', is a direct consequence — the returned map loses its type as soon as it enters the response map.
DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, and Edge are all well-typed domain classes that already contain the needed fields; they could be serialized directly.
issues.suggestion: Define typed response record classes (e.g., ExploreResponse, SeedCollocateEntry) or annotate the existing domain records with the JSON serialization library's annotations so fastjson2 can serialize them directly; this would eliminate the format* helpers and the unchecked cast.

root_causes: RelationConfig is a Java record, which syntactically looks non-nullable, but one component (relationType) was intentionally made nullable to represent 'absent'; this mismatch between Java's structural promise and the actual contract propagates to call sites that assume non-null.

The API layer was built incrementally with ad-hoc Map<String, Object> response building rather than defining typed response objects upfront, leading to type erasure at the boundary and the associated unchecked-cast smell.
likely_symptoms: relation_type_nullable_npe — NPE only manifests when a grammar config omits the relation_type field, which may be rare in practice but untested
get_full_pattern_null_propagation — null pattern paths are guarded at construction time in GrammarConfigLoader, so this fires only if RelationConfig is instantiated outside the loader
